company_persona: a vendor of embedded AI support agents
title: The refund policy that never existed
prompt: You're the PM for Sable, an AI support agent that companies embed in their help centers. This morning your largest customer — an airline — escalated: Sable told a passenger they were entitled to a full cash refund plus compensation, citing a policy that doesn't exist. The passenger posted screenshots and it's picking up traction. Walk me through what you do — today, this week, and structurally.

hidden_facts.customer_action: The airline honored the refund to contain the story and is furious. They are 18% of ARR with a renewal in four months.
hidden_facts.blast_radius: A log search finds 214 conversations in the last 30 days where Sable cited a 'policy' string that matches no knowledge-base document — about 0.3% of policy-related chats, concentrated on this airline.
hidden_facts.root_cause: The airline rewrote its refund KB article five weeks ago. The per-tenant reindexing job has been silently failing for them since then, so retrieval served stale chunks and the model filled the gaps.
hidden_facts.model_changes: No model or prompt version changed in this window.
hidden_facts.current_guardrails: Answers show a citation link, but nothing enforces that the answer is actually grounded in the cited document before sending.
hidden_facts.groundedness_option: A groundedness-check gate (verify the answer is supported by retrieved text before sending) adds ~300ms latency and ~8% inference cost.
hidden_facts.strict_mode_option: A strict quote-only mode for policy questions drops the answer rate (deflection) by ~25% — a real cost, since deflection is what customers pay for.
hidden_facts.monitoring_gap: There is no per-tenant index-freshness or indexing-job-health alert. Nobody was paged.
hidden_facts.contract: The SLA contains 'commercially reasonable accuracy' language; legal says exposure is manageable but the relationship is the real risk.
hidden_facts.competitor: A rival has started marketing 'hallucination-proof' guarantees to enterprise buyers.

ideal_answer_notes: Contain before diagnosing: today, switch the affected tenant's policy-class queries to strict quote-only mode (accepting the deflection hit), get comms and legal aligned with the airline, and support the customer publicly — don't debug in public.
Scope the blast radius with data before generalizing: the 214-conversation search, which tenants, which intent classes — and proactively check other tenants for the same failure signature.

Root-cause using the RAG pipeline MECE: retrieval (stale index — the actual cause), generation (no model change), knowledge-base content, prompt/config. Confirm with the timeline: KB rewrite five weeks ago plus silently failing reindex job matches the incident window.

Make the precise diagnosis explicitly: this is not 'the model hallucinated' as a mystery — the system served stale grounding and the model papered over the gap. The distinction drives both the fix and the customer narrative.

Fix in layers with stated tradeoffs: per-tenant index-health alerts (cheap, do immediately); groundedness gating for high-stakes intents (+300ms/+8% cost — worth it for policy/commitment answers, probably not for everything); citation-or-refuse for anything that commits the customer to money or obligations.
Introduce risk tiers per intent: refund/legal/medical/billing answers get strict grounding and lower autonomy; chit-chat and how-to answers keep the fast path. One global setting overcorrects.

Define guardrail metrics that become launch gates: groundedness rate on sampled answers, unsupported-claim rate from human audits, index freshness age per tenant, and time-to-detect for pipeline failures.
Handle the relationship deliberately: a written incident report with the real root cause, the remediation plan with dates, and success criteria tied to the four-month renewal.

Structural follow-through: add stale-KB scenarios to the eval suite, chaos-test the indexer, and consider making groundedness reporting a customer-visible trust feature — turning the incident into the counter to the rival's 'hallucination-proof' pitch.
extra_checklist: Contains the incident for the affected tenant before starting the broad diagnosis
Locates the failure precisely in the retrieval layer (stale index) instead of stopping at 'the model hallucinated'
Proposes risk-tiered guardrails with their latency, cost, and deflection tradeoffs stated as numbers

anchors.strong: Contains today with strict mode plus customer comms, scopes with the log search, walks the retrieval-vs-generation-vs-content split and confirms stale indexing via the timeline, then ships layered fixes — freshness alerts, groundedness gates on risk-tiered intents, citation-or-refuse for commitments — with the 300ms/8%/25% tradeoffs weighed and renewal-focused customer handling.
anchors.at_bar: Handles the customer and proposes checking 'what the AI retrieved', reaching the stale-content cause with a nudge or two, and suggests guardrails, but treats the fixes as absolutes without weighing the latency/cost/deflection numbers or tiering by intent risk.
anchors.weak: Jumps to 'models hallucinate, we need a better model or better prompts', never asks what changed or searches the logs, proposes a disclaimer as the fix, and ignores the renewal, the monitoring gap, and the difference between retrieval and generation failures.
